# Synapse Notebook — SalesLT Silver Cleaning
# ============================================
# Each cell = one cleaning rule. Toggle any cell off by commenting out the
# function call at the bottom of that cell.
# All rules are applied to every table in TABLE_CONFIGS.
# Run cells top-to-bottom. Each rule reads `silver_dfs` and writes back to it.
#
# USAGE:
#   1. Run CELL 0  (config)
#   2. Run CELL 1  (load from Bronze)
#   3. Run CELL 2–11 in order  (cleaning rules)
#   4. Run CELL 12 (write to Silver)
#   5. Run CELL 13 (validation report)


# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# CELL 0 — CONFIGURATION
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

STORAGE_ACCOUNT = "storageproject12026"
BRONZE_ROOT = f"abfss://bronze@{STORAGE_ACCOUNT}.dfs.core.windows.net/SalesLT/"
SILVER_ROOT = f"abfss://silver@{STORAGE_ACCOUNT}.dfs.core.windows.net/SalesLT/"

# ── Per-table config ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# casing        : { col_name_after_snake_case: "lower" | "upper" | "title" }
# required_cols : used for NULL check (numeric validation cell)
# numeric_cols  : { col_name: "non_negative" | "positive" }
# business_keys : columns that form the unique business key

TABLE_CONFIGS = {
    "Customer": {
        "casing":        {"email_address": "lower",
                          "first_name": "title", "last_name": "title"},
        "required_cols": ["customer_id", "first_name", "last_name", "email_address"],
        "numeric_cols":  {},
        "business_keys": ["customer_id"],
    },
    "Address": {
        "casing":        {"city": "title", "country_region": "upper",
                          "state_province": "upper"},
        "required_cols": ["address_id", "address_line1", "city",
                          "state_province", "country_region", "postal_code"],
        "numeric_cols":  {},
        "business_keys": ["address_id"],
    },
    "ProductCategory": {
        "casing":        {"name": "title"},
        "required_cols": ["product_category_id", "name"],
        "numeric_cols":  {},
        "business_keys": ["product_category_id"],
    },
    "Product": {
        "casing":        {"color": "title", "size": "upper"},
        "required_cols": ["product_id", "name", "product_number",
                          "list_price", "product_category_id"],
        "numeric_cols":  {"list_price": "non_negative",
                          "standard_cost": "non_negative"},
        "business_keys": ["product_id"],
    },
    "CustomerAddress": {
        "casing":        {"address_type": "title"},
        "required_cols": ["customer_id", "address_id", "address_type"],
        "numeric_cols":  {},
        "business_keys": ["customer_id", "address_id"],
    },
    "SalesOrderHeader": {
        "casing":        {},
        "required_cols": ["sales_order_id", "order_date", "customer_id",
                          "sub_total", "tax_amt", "freight", "total_due"],
        "numeric_cols":  {"sub_total": "non_negative", "tax_amt": "non_negative",
                          "freight": "non_negative", "total_due": "non_negative"},
        "business_keys": ["sales_order_id"],
    },
    "SalesOrderDetail": {
        "casing":        {},
        "required_cols": ["sales_order_id", "sales_order_detail_id",
                          "product_id", "order_qty", "unit_price", "line_total"],
        "numeric_cols":  {"order_qty": "positive", "unit_price": "non_negative",
                          "line_total": "non_negative"},
        "business_keys": ["sales_order_id", "sales_order_detail_id"],
    },
}

print("✓ Config loaded")
print(f"  Tables : {list(TABLE_CONFIGS.keys())}")
print(f"  Bronze : {BRONZE_ROOT}")
print(f"  Silver : {SILVER_ROOT}")


# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# CELL 1 — LOAD ALL TABLES FROM BRONZE
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

from pyspark.sql import DataFrame
from pyspark.sql import functions as F
from pyspark.sql.types import StringType, TimestampType, DateType

# silver_dfs holds the working DataFrames — all cleaning rules mutate this dict
silver_dfs: dict[str, DataFrame] = {}

for table in TABLE_CONFIGS:
    path = BRONZE_ROOT + table + "/"
    silver_dfs[table] = spark.read.format("parquet").load(path)
    print(f"✓ Loaded {table:30s} — {silver_dfs[table].count():>6,} rows  "
          f"| {len(silver_dfs[table].columns)} cols")


# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# CELL 2 — RULE 1 : Convert column names to snake_case
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# WHY  : Uniform naming convention across all tables.
# HOW  : PySpark — regex-based rename on every column.
# SQL  : n/a (renaming is a schema operation, not a data operation).

import re

def _to_snake(name: str) -> str:
    name = re.sub(r'([a-z0-9])([A-Z])',   r'\1_\2', name)   # camelCase humps
    name = re.sub(r'([A-Z]+)([A-Z][a-z])', r'\1_\2', name)  # ACRONYMWord
    name = re.sub(r'[\s\-]+', '_', name)                      # spaces / dashes
    name = re.sub(r'_+', '_', name)                           # collapse repeats
    return name.strip('_').lower()

def apply_snake_case(dfs: dict) -> dict:
    for table, df in dfs.items():
        renamed = {col: _to_snake(col) for col in df.columns if _to_snake(col) != col}
        for old, new in renamed.items():
            df = df.withColumnRenamed(old, new)
        dfs[table] = df
        if renamed:
            print(f"  [{table}] renamed: {renamed}")
    return dfs

silver_dfs = apply_snake_case(silver_dfs)
print("✓ RULE 1 — snake_case applied")


# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# CELL 3 — RULE 2 : Convert date / timestamp columns to standard format
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# WHY  : Consistent ISO-8601 dates make downstream SQL comparisons safe.
# HOW  : PySpark detects column type; SQL FORMAT_TIMESTAMP / DATE_FORMAT used
#        via selectExpr for readability.
# NOTE : Timestamps  → "yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss"
#        Dates        → "yyyy-MM-dd"
#        String cols named *date* → attempt coercion

DATE_FMT      = "yyyy-MM-dd"
DATETIME_FMT  = "yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss"

def apply_date_formatting(dfs: dict) -> dict:
    for table, df in dfs.items():
        for field in df.schema.fields:
            col, dtype = field.name, field.dataType
            if isinstance(dtype, TimestampType):
                # SQL-style via selectExpr for transparency
                df = df.withColumn(
                    col,
                    F.date_format(F.from_utc_timestamp(F.col(col), "UTC"), DATETIME_FMT)
                )
            elif isinstance(dtype, DateType):
                df = df.withColumn(col, F.date_format(F.col(col), DATE_FMT))
            elif isinstance(dtype, StringType) and "date" in col.lower():
                df = df.withColumn(
                    col,
                    F.date_format(F.to_timestamp(F.col(col)), DATE_FMT)
                )
        dfs[table] = df
    return dfs

silver_dfs = apply_date_formatting(silver_dfs)
print("✓ RULE 2 — date/timestamp formatting applied")


# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# CELL 4 — RULE 3 : Trim string columns
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# WHY  : Leading/trailing spaces cause invisible duplicates and broken joins.
# HOW  : SQL TRIM() applied to every StringType column via selectExpr.

def apply_trim(dfs: dict) -> dict:
    for table, df in dfs.items():
        exprs = []
        for field in df.schema.fields:
            if isinstance(field.dataType, StringType):
                exprs.append(f"TRIM(`{field.name}`) AS `{field.name}`")
            else:
                exprs.append(f"`{field.name}`")
        dfs[table] = df.selectExpr(*exprs)
    return dfs

silver_dfs = apply_trim(silver_dfs)
print("✓ RULE 3 — string trim applied")


# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# CELL 5 — RULE 4 : Convert empty strings to NULL
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# WHY  : Empty strings are semantically NULL but break IS NULL checks.
# HOW  : SQL NULLIF(col, '') — returns NULL when value equals empty string.

def apply_empty_to_null(dfs: dict) -> dict:
    for table, df in dfs.items():
        exprs = []
        for field in df.schema.fields:
            if isinstance(field.dataType, StringType):
                exprs.append(f"NULLIF(`{field.name}`, '') AS `{field.name}`")
            else:
                exprs.append(f"`{field.name}`")
        dfs[table] = df.selectExpr(*exprs)
    return dfs

silver_dfs = apply_empty_to_null(silver_dfs)
print("✓ RULE 4 — empty strings → NULL applied")


# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# CELL 6 — RULE 5 : Remove duplicate spaces
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# WHY  : "John  Smith" ≠ "John Smith" — breaks name matching and display.
# HOW  : SQL REGEXP_REPLACE with \s{2,} → single space.

def apply_remove_dup_spaces(dfs: dict) -> dict:
    for table, df in dfs.items():
        exprs = []
        for field in df.schema.fields:
            if isinstance(field.dataType, StringType):
                exprs.append(
                    f"REGEXP_REPLACE(`{field.name}`, '\\\\s{{2,}}', ' ') "
                    f"AS `{field.name}`"
                )
            else:
                exprs.append(f"`{field.name}`")
        dfs[table] = df.selectExpr(*exprs)
    return dfs

silver_dfs = apply_remove_dup_spaces(silver_dfs)
print("✓ RULE 5 — duplicate spaces removed")


# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# CELL 7 — RULE 6 : Normalize special characters (strip accents)
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# WHY  : "Müller" and "Muller" should match; accents cause silent mismatches.
# HOW  : Python UDF using unicodedata NFKD decomposition + combining char removal.
#        (No native Spark SQL equivalent — UDF is the right tool here.)

import unicodedata
from pyspark.sql.types import StringType as ST

@F.udf(ST())
def _strip_accents(value):
    if value is None:
        return None
    nfkd = unicodedata.normalize("NFKD", value)
    return "".join(c for c in nfkd if not unicodedata.combining(c))

def apply_normalize_special_chars(dfs: dict) -> dict:
    for table, df in dfs.items():
        for field in df.schema.fields:
            if isinstance(field.dataType, StringType):
                df = df.withColumn(field.name, _strip_accents(F.col(field.name)))
        dfs[table] = df
    return dfs

silver_dfs = apply_normalize_special_chars(silver_dfs)
print("✓ RULE 6 — special characters normalized")


# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# CELL 8 — RULE 7 : Standardize casing for selected columns
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# WHY  : "CANADA", "canada", "Canada" are the same value — must be uniform.
# HOW  : SQL UPPER / LOWER / INITCAP per column, driven by TABLE_CONFIGS.

_SQL_CASING = {"upper": "UPPER", "lower": "LOWER", "title": "INITCAP"}

def apply_casing(dfs: dict, configs: dict) -> dict:
    for table, df in dfs.items():
        casing_map = configs[table].get("casing", {})
        for col_name, mode in casing_map.items():
            if col_name in df.columns and mode in _SQL_CASING:
                fn = _SQL_CASING[mode]
                df = df.withColumn(col_name, F.expr(f"{fn}(`{col_name}`)"))
                print(f"  [{table}] {fn}({col_name})")
        dfs[table] = df
    return dfs

silver_dfs = apply_casing(silver_dfs, TABLE_CONFIGS)
print("✓ RULE 7 — casing standardized")


# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# CELL 9 — RULE 8 : Keep meaningful nullable optional fields
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# WHY  : Optional fields (middle_name, color, comment …) are legitimately NULL.
#        This cell adds a helper flag so downstream users know a row has
#        optional data filled in — without ever dropping valid NULLs.
# HOW  : PySpark — OR-chain across optional cols → _has_optional_data boolean.
#        Uses required_cols from config; everything else is treated as optional.

def apply_optional_field_flag(dfs: dict, configs: dict) -> dict:
    for table, df in dfs.items():
        required = set(configs[table].get("required_cols", []))
        optional = [c for c in df.columns if c not in required]
        if not optional:
            dfs[table] = df
            continue
        has_data = F.lit(False)
        for c in optional:
            has_data = has_data | F.col(c).isNotNull()
        dfs[table] = df.withColumn("_has_optional_data", has_data)
        print(f"  [{table}] optional cols flagged: {optional}")
    return dfs

silver_dfs = apply_optional_field_flag(silver_dfs, TABLE_CONFIGS)
print("✓ RULE 8 — nullable optional field flag added")


# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# CELL 10 — RULE 9 : Check duplicate business keys
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# WHY  : Duplicate PKs corrupt joins and aggregations.
# HOW  : SQL GROUP BY + HAVING COUNT(*) > 1 via createOrReplaceTempView,
#        then PySpark left-semi join to flag the offending rows.
#        Rows are KEPT — a _dq_duplicate_key column is added (True = bad row).

def apply_duplicate_key_check(dfs: dict, configs: dict) -> dict:
    for table, df in dfs.items():
        bk = configs[table].get("business_keys", [])
        if not bk:
            continue

        # Register temp view so we can write the check in readable SQL
        view = f"_tmp_{table}"
        df.createOrReplaceTempView(view)

        bk_sql = ", ".join(f"`{c}`" for c in bk)
        dup_sql = f"""
            SELECT {bk_sql}
            FROM   {view}
            GROUP  BY {bk_sql}
            HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
        """
        dup_keys = spark.sql(dup_sql)

        if dup_keys.count() == 0:
            df = df.withColumn("_dq_duplicate_key", F.lit(False))
            print(f"  [{table}] 0 duplicate keys — clean ✓")
        else:
            # Flag via left-semi join
            is_dup = df.join(dup_keys, on=bk, how="left_semi") \
                       .select(*bk) \
                       .distinct()
            dup_rows = is_dup.collect()
            dup_cond = F.lit(False)
            for row in dup_rows:
                row_cond = F.lit(True)
                for c in bk:
                    row_cond = row_cond & (F.col(c) == row[c])
                dup_cond = dup_cond | row_cond
            df = df.withColumn("_dq_duplicate_key", dup_cond)
            n = df.filter(F.col("_dq_duplicate_key")).count()
            print(f"  [{table}] ⚠  {n} rows flagged with duplicate key on {bk}")

        dfs[table] = df
    return dfs

silver_dfs = apply_duplicate_key_check(silver_dfs, TABLE_CONFIGS)
print("✓ RULE 9 — duplicate business key check done")


# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# CELL 11 — RULE 10 : Apply numeric validations
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# WHY  : Negative prices or zero quantities are data errors, not business logic.
# HOW  : SQL CASE expression per column:
#          non_negative → value < 0  is invalid
#          positive     → value <= 0 is invalid
#        Adds _dq_invalid_<col> flag column per failing column.
#        Also flags NULLs in required numeric cols.

def apply_numeric_validations(dfs: dict, configs: dict) -> dict:
    for table, df in dfs.items():
        numeric_map  = configs[table].get("numeric_cols",  {})
        required     = set(configs[table].get("required_cols", []))

        # NULL check on required numeric cols
        for col_name in required:
            if col_name in df.columns:
                field = [f for f in df.schema.fields if f.name == col_name]
                if field and not isinstance(field[0].dataType, StringType):
                    null_flag = f"_dq_null_{col_name}"
                    df = df.withColumn(null_flag, F.col(col_name).isNull())
                    n = df.filter(F.col(null_flag)).count()
                    if n:
                        print(f"  [{table}] ⚠  {n} NULLs in required col '{col_name}'")

        # Range checks
        for col_name, rule in numeric_map.items():
            if col_name not in df.columns:
                continue
            flag = f"_dq_invalid_{col_name}"
            if rule == "non_negative":
                # SQL: CASE WHEN col < 0 THEN true ELSE false END
                df = df.withColumn(flag, F.expr(f"`{col_name}` < 0"))
            elif rule == "positive":
                df = df.withColumn(flag, F.expr(f"`{col_name}` <= 0"))

            n = df.filter(F.col(flag)).count()
            symbol = "⚠ " if n else "✓"
            print(f"  [{table}] {symbol} {rule} check on '{col_name}': {n} violations")

        dfs[table] = df
    return dfs

silver_dfs = apply_numeric_validations(silver_dfs, TABLE_CONFIGS)
print("✓ RULE 10 — numeric validations done")


# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# CELL 12 — WRITE CLEANED TABLES TO SILVER (Delta)
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

for table, df in silver_dfs.items():
    path = SILVER_ROOT + table + "/"
    df.write.format("delta").mode("overwrite").save(path)
    print(f"✓ Written [{table}] → {path}")

print("\n✓ All tables written to Silver")


# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# CELL 13 — VALIDATION REPORT
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Summary of all _dq_* flag columns across every table.
# Run this after CELL 12 to confirm what was written.

print("=" * 65)
print(f"{'TABLE':<25} {'FLAG COLUMN':<35} {'FLAGGED':>7}")
print("=" * 65)

total_issues = 0
for table, df in silver_dfs.items():
    dq_cols = [c for c in df.columns if c.startswith("_dq_")]
    if not dq_cols:
        print(f"  {table:<23} — no DQ flags")
        continue
    for dq_col in dq_cols:
        n = df.filter(F.col(dq_col) == True).count()
        symbol = "⚠ " if n else "✓ "
        print(f"  {table:<23} {symbol}{dq_col:<33} {n:>7,}")
        total_issues += n

print("=" * 65)
print(f"  Total flagged rows across all checks: {total_issues:,}")
print("=" * 65)
